primary_diagnosis.name: Overflow faecal incontinence secondary to rectal faecal impaction (opioid-induced constipation)

pathophysiology_summary: A regular opioid slows colonic transit and, without a co-prescribed aperient, allows a hard faecal mass to accumulate in the rectum. Liquid stool from higher in the colon then tracks around the impacted mass and leaks out under a lax sphincter (overflow) — so the 'diarrhoea' is a sign of constipation, not infection. Reduced anal sphincter tone, blunted rectal sensation and immobility in a frail, cognitively impaired elder remove the normal continence safeguards. Prolonged contact of liquid stool with skin causes moisture-associated dermatitis and excoriation, which without protection can progress to erosion and secondary bacterial infection. Unrelieved impaction can progress to functional large-bowel obstruction and, rarely, stercoral ulceration/perforation; reduced oral intake risks dehydration and acute kidney injury.

differential.diagnosis: Overflow faecal incontinence secondary to faecal impaction
differential.evidence_basis: Reduced bowel frequency and bloating followed by continuous watery leakage in an immobile elder on an opioid without aperient is the classic overflow pattern.
Continuous watery output superficially resembles diarrhoea — the key red herring — but there is no fever, no hyperactive bowel sounds and no acute onset of true diarrhoea.
Altered bowel habit in an older adult mandates consideration; needs PR exam and lower-GI assessment to exclude a mass, but no PR bleeding, weight loss or distinct palpable mass are reported.
No gross rectal bleeding and no prior relapsing inflammatory abdominal pain make this unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognising the overflow pattern over the infective-diarrhoea red herring is the pivotal early step.

differential.diagnosis: Overflow faecal incontinence secondary to faecal impaction (opioid-induced)
differential.evidence_basis: Opioid without aperient + preceding reduced frequency + bloating + continuous overflow leakage now dominates the picture.
Afebrile, no rigors, watery leakage rather than true frequent diarrhoeal stools; pattern is overflow, not infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The opioid-without-aperient link converts the differential to a mechanistic near-certainty pending PR confirmation.

differential.diagnosis: Overflow faecal incontinence secondary to rectal faecal impaction
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed clinically by digital rectal examination (palpable impaction with overflow, reduced sphincter tone) — a hands-on step the AI cannot perform remotely.
Remains on the fast-track safety-net until an in-person exam and, if indicated, lower-GI investigation exclude it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The final answer requires an in-person PR exam; telehealth can reach a strong presumptive diagnosis but not confirm it.

red_flags.description: Abdominal distension with risk of progression to large-bowel obstruction from unrelieved impaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Impaction can progress to functional obstruction; cessation of flatus/stool, worsening distension, severe pain or vomiting are emergency signs.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed evolving obstruction can lead to stercoral ulceration or perforation.

red_flags.description: Systemic infection / sepsis in a frail elder (fever >=38, rigors, new confusion)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Frail elders under-report and present atypically; a new fever or acute confusion is a sepsis-until-proven-otherwise emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised sepsis (including from secondary skin infection) carries high mortality.

red_flags.description: Painful perianal skin breakdown with redness spreading toward the buttocks — risk of secondary cellulitis / tissue breakdown
red_flags.clinical_significance: Moisture-associated skin damage can progress to erosion and cellulitis; rapidly spreading redness needs same-day in-person review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive tissue necrosis and secondary bacterial cellulitis.

red_flags.description: Reduced oral intake in a frail elder — dehydration and acute kidney injury risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reduced intake plus fluid loss through overflow leakage risks dehydration/AKI; obtundation or no urine output are emergency signs.

red_flags.description: Occult colorectal malignancy masquerading as altered bowel habit
red_flags.clinical_significance: New change in bowel habit in an older adult requires PR exam and, if indicated, lower-GI assessment to exclude malignancy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Mistaking overflow liquid leakage for true diarrhoea and treating with an antidiarrhoeal (e.g. loperamide), which worsens the impaction and can precipitate obstruction.
Ordering stool cultures / advising isolation and rehydration for 'gastro' while missing the impacted rectum that is driving the leakage.
Failing to connect the regular opioid with no co-prescribed aperient to the constipation and impaction.
Attempting to instruct the caregiver in home manual disimpaction — this requires a hands-on PR examination and procedure the AI cannot perform or supervise safely.
Dismissing the picture as 'just incontinence in a dementia patient' and thereby missing obstruction, sepsis, dehydration or malignancy.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Digital rectal examination — palpable rectal faecal impaction, reduced anal sphincter tone and voluntary squeeze pressure, altered perianal sensation, loss of the anocutaneous reflex
Abdominal palpation and percussion — degree of distension, tenderness, masses
Formal staging and extent of the perianal excoriation / moisture-associated skin damage
Endoanal ultrasound for anal sphincter defects
Bloods (U&E/creatinine/eGFR for AKI, FBE/CRP for infection) and stool studies
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (after-hours home visit / GP / community nursing) for a rectal examination, manual disimpaction and perianal skin/continence care; recognise overflow rather than infection; give a conservative safety-net for obstruction, sepsis, dehydration and occult malignancy. Do not attempt to treat as diarrhoea and do not instruct home disimpaction.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is mislabelling the overflow leakage as infective diarrhoea and managing it as gastroenteritis, or under-acting on a stable-appearing but frail elder who can decompensate.

guidelines_referenced.reference: eTG / RACGP guidance on faecal impaction and opioid-induced constipation in older adults
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise overflow incontinence as a sign of impaction; disimpact and establish a regular bowel regimen; co-prescribe an aperient with any opioid; avoid antidiarrhoeals.
